Category: --- BIM AUTHORING MODELS: the 3D digital representations of what is being built ---

Description: A parametric digital representation of the physical and functional characteristics of a building — the authoritative geometric and data source for design, construction coordination, and facility operations. Organized by discipline: architectural, structural, MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing), civil. Each discipline model is authored in a BIM tool and contains model elements with type, material, dimensions, location, and relationship properties. The model is the source of truth for quantities, coordination, and visualization. Lifecycle: Concept → Schematic Design → Design Development → Construction Documents → As-Built.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (BIM and Digital Data Protocol); ISO 19650 (BIM Framework); BIM Execution Plan (BEP); National BIM Standard
Common Values: Architectural Model, Structural Model, Mechanical Model, Electrical Model, Plumbing Model, Fire Protection Model, Civil Model, Landscape Model
Source Systems: Revit (Autodesk); ArchiCAD (Graphisoft); Tekla Structures (Trimble); Bentley OpenBuildings; Vectorworks; Allplan
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not host or import BIM authoring models. Models are viewed in Procore via embedded viewers but model data is not structured in Procore tables.
Benchmarking Use: Compare model discipline coverage and authoring tool usage across project types
Notes: BIM models are the richest source of Building Element data — every element has type, material, dimensions, location, and spatial relationships. The REPRESENTS relationship bridges Models & BIM to Building Elements. Cross-domain: model → drawings → RFIs → change events.

Description: A combined multi-discipline model assembled from individual discipline models — the integrated view used for coordination, clash detection, and owner reviews. Created by aggregating models from architect, structural engineer, MEP engineers, and trade contractors into a single spatial environment. Federated models are reference-only (not editable) and must be refreshed as discipline models are updated.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (multi-discipline coordination); ISO 19650 (Common Data Environment); BIM Execution Plan
Common Values: Full federation (all disciplines), Partial federation (MEP only, structure + MEP), Trade-specific coordination sets
Source Systems: Navisworks (Autodesk); Solibri; BIM Track; BIM Collab; Trimble Connect; BIM 360 Glue; Procore (model viewer)
Procore Coverage: None — Procore provides model viewing but does not store or manage federated model data. Federation happens in coordination tools outside Procore.
Benchmarking Use: Compare federated model update frequency and coordination cycle duration across project types
Notes: Federation quality depends on model exchange format (IFC, NWC, RVT) and update cadence. Weekly federation with detection is standard; daily on fast-track projects. Cross-domain: federated models are the input for clash detection and coordination issues.

Description: A 3D model linked to the project schedule — visualizes construction sequence over time by associating model elements with schedule activities. Enables construction phasing visualization, logistics planning, and progress tracking. Each model element or group is linked to a schedule activity that defines when it is installed. Lifecycle: Created during preconstruction → Updated as schedule changes → Used during construction for planning.
Industry Standard: BIM Execution Plan phasing requirements; AGC BIMForum LOD Spec (for construction-phase modeling)
Common Values: Phase visualization, Logistics simulation, Progress comparison (planned vs. actual), Safety sequence review, Crane placement planning
Source Systems: Synchro (Bentley); Navisworks; Fuzor; Autodesk Construction Cloud; Primavera (P6 integration); MS Project
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not support 4D model linking. Schedule data exists in Procore (scheduling tables) and models are viewable, but the linkage between model elements and schedule activities is managed in external tools.
Benchmarking Use: Compare 4D model adoption rates across project types and complexity levels
Notes: 4D models bridge the Models & BIM domain to the Schedule domain. Most valuable on complex projects with phasing constraints. Adoption is growing but still limited to ~15-25% of major projects. Cross-domain: schedule activities → model elements → building elements.

Description: A 3D model linked to cost data — enables quantity-based estimating and cost visualization by associating model elements with cost information (cost codes, unit prices, assemblies). Quantities extracted from the model drive the estimate. Lifecycle: Created during estimating → Updated as design evolves → Reconciled against bid amounts.
Industry Standard: AACE cost estimating practices; BIM-based estimating workflows; RICS (quantity surveying)
Common Values: Quantity takeoff from model, Assembly-based costing, Cost visualization by system, Value engineering analysis
Source Systems: Sage Estimating; CostX (Exactal); Cubicost; Revit quantity schedules; Assemble (Autodesk); iTWO (RIB)
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not support 5D cost-model linking. Budget and commitment data exist in Procore financial tables, but model-to-cost linkage is managed in estimating tools.
Benchmarking Use: Compare model-based estimating accuracy (model quantity vs. actual installed) across project types
Notes: 5D models bridge Models & BIM to Financial Instruments. Model quantities → cost estimates → budgets → actuals is the full financial lifecycle. Most GCs still estimate separately from the model — 5D adoption is limited to larger/more sophisticated firms.

Description: A BIM model updated to reflect actual constructed conditions — incorporates field modifications, deviations from design, and final installed locations. Created during closeout by updating the design model with as-built survey data, field markups, and contractor input. Delivered to the owner as part of closeout documentation for facility management and future renovations.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (as-built modeling requirements); contract-specific BIM deliverables; COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange)
Common Values: Updated architectural model, Updated structural model, Updated MEP models, Reality capture-verified model, COBie data export
Source Systems: Revit; Navisworks; point cloud software (Leica Cyclone, Trimble RealWorks); reality capture platforms
Procore Coverage: None — as-built models are not stored in Procore. As-built documentation (drawings, PDFs) may be uploaded but the model itself lives in BIM authoring tools.
Benchmarking Use: Compare as-built model delivery rates and completeness across project types
Notes: As-built models are a major closeout deliverable but adoption is inconsistent. Many projects still deliver 2D as-built drawings (redlines) rather than updated 3D models. Cross-domain: as-built models represent the final state of Building Elements and connect to closeout Documents.

Category: --- MODEL ELEMENTS & PROPERTIES: the individual components and their data within a BIM model ---

Description: An individual component within a BIM model — a wall, beam, column, pipe, duct, fixture, or any other discrete building component with geometry, type, material, and location properties. Model elements are the atomic units of BIM — each one represents a specific physical thing that will be built. Element properties include: type/family, material, dimensions (length, width, height, diameter), location (level, grid, coordinates), and relationships to other elements (hosted by, connected to, adjacent to).
Industry Standard: IFC entity types (IfcWall, IfcBeam, IfcPipeSegment, etc.); Revit family/type system; LOD Specification
Common Values: Walls, Columns, Beams, Slabs, Doors, Windows, Pipes, Ducts, Cable Trays, Fixtures, Equipment, Fittings, Accessories
Source Systems: Revit; ArchiCAD; Tekla; MEP authoring tools (Fabrication CADmep, AutoCAD MEP)
Procore Coverage: None — individual model elements are not stored in Procore. Procore references model elements indirectly through drawings, coordination issues, and observations that cite specific components.
Benchmarking Use: Model element counts by type and system enable quantity benchmarking (elements per SF) across project types
Notes: Model elements are the bridge between the digital model and physical Building Elements. Each model element REPRESENTS a building element. IFC entity types provide the classification. Element properties (material, dimensions) drive quantity takeoff and cost estimation.

Description: A specification that defines the completeness and reliability of a model element at a given project phase — governs what information can be extracted from the model. LOD 100 (conceptual), LOD 200 (approximate geometry), LOD 300 (precise geometry for coordination), LOD 350 (coordination with other systems), LOD 400 (fabrication-ready), LOD 500 (as-built verified). Each LOD level defines what geometry, data, and relationships are reliable. LOD governs which model uses are valid at each phase.
Industry Standard: AIA E203; BIMForum LOD Specification (Level of Development); ISO 19650 (information requirements)
Common Values: LOD 100 (concept), LOD 200 (approx geometry), LOD 300 (precise geometry), LOD 350 (coordination), LOD 400 (fabrication), LOD 500 (as-built verified)
Source Systems: BIM Execution Plans; Revit (model element detail); BIMForum LOD Spec documentation
Procore Coverage: None — LOD is a modeling specification, not tracked in Procore. LOD requirements are defined in the BIM Execution Plan (a document in the Documents domain).
Benchmarking Use: Compare LOD specification requirements across project types and delivery methods
Notes: LOD 300/350 is the minimum for reliable coordination and clash detection. LOD 400 enables prefabrication. LOD requirements affect modeling cost and schedule. Cross-domain: LOD governs what quantities can be reliably extracted for Financial Instruments.

Description: Material quantities extracted from model elements — counts, areas, volumes, lengths, and weights derived from model geometry and type properties. Quantities drive estimating (5D), procurement, and production tracking. Model-based takeoff is more accurate and faster than manual takeoff from 2D drawings but depends on model quality and LOD.
Industry Standard: AACE quantity surveying practices; RICS New Rules of Measurement; BIM-based QTO workflows
Common Values: Element counts, Linear quantities (LF of pipe, duct, conduit), Area quantities (SF of wall, floor, ceiling), Volume quantities (CY of concrete), Weight quantities (tons of steel)
Source Systems: CostX; Bluebeam (2D takeoff); Assemble; Revit quantity schedules; On-Screen Takeoff; PlanSwift; Sage Estimating
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not perform or store quantity takeoffs. Quantities may be reflected in budget line items and commitment line items as bid quantities, but the takeoff process is external.
Benchmarking Use: Compare model-based vs. manual takeoff accuracy and speed across project types
Notes: Quantity takeoff bridges Models & BIM to Financial Instruments (cost estimates) and Resources (material quantities). Takeoff accuracy depends on LOD — LOD 300+ needed for reliable quantities. Discrepancy between model quantities and installed quantities reveals design vs. field gaps.

Description: The non-geometric data attached to model elements — manufacturer, model number, fire rating, acoustic rating, energy performance, warranty period, maintenance requirements, and other attribute data. Model properties make BIM more than geometry — they carry the information needed for specification compliance, facility management, and lifecycle analysis. COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange) is the standard format for extracting model properties for facility handover.
Industry Standard: COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange); IFC property sets; Revit shared parameters; ISO 19650 information requirements
Common Values: Manufacturer, Model Number, Fire Rating, Acoustic Rating, U-Value, Finish, Color, Warranty Period, Maintenance Interval, Commissioning Status
Source Systems: Revit (shared/project parameters); COBie export tools; asset management systems (Archibus, FM:Systems); Maximo
Procore Coverage: None — model properties are not stored in Procore. Some properties may appear in submittal data (product data submittals) or specification sections, but the structured model property data lives in BIM tools.
Benchmarking Use: Compare model property completeness (% of elements with required COBie data) across project types
Notes: Model properties are the data pipeline to facility management. Complete model properties at handover eliminate the need for the owner to manually catalog building assets. Cross-domain: properties map to specification requirements (Documents), maintenance needs (post-construction Phases), and equipment data.

Category: --- COORDINATION & CLASH DETECTION: using models to identify and resolve spatial conflicts ---

Description: An identified spatial conflict between model elements from different disciplines — a pipe running through a beam, a duct intersecting a structural column, or conflicting MEP routes. Clash detection is automated (Navisworks, Solibri) and produces clash reports with geometry, location, and the involved elements/disciplines. Clashes must be resolved before construction to avoid field rework. Types: hard clash (physical intersection), soft clash (clearance violation), workflow clash (sequencing conflict).
Industry Standard: BIM Execution Plan clash detection protocols; AGC BIMForum coordination guidelines
Common Values: Hard Clash (physical intersection), Soft Clash (clearance/tolerance violation), Workflow Clash (sequence conflict); Status: New, Active, Reviewed, Resolved, Approved
Source Systems: Navisworks (Autodesk); Solibri; BIM Track; BIM Collab; Trimble Connect; BIM 360 Glue
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not perform or store clash detection results. Clash findings that require action become coordination issues (which ARE in Procore).
Benchmarking Use: Compare clash counts per discipline pair and resolution time across project types to benchmark coordination effectiveness
Notes: Clash detection results are the input — coordination issues are the output. Not all clashes become coordination issues (many are resolved in the coordination meeting). Clash volume by discipline pair (structural-mechanical, electrical-plumbing) reveals design coordination quality.

Description: A design or constructability problem identified during BIM coordination review — the actionable output of clash detection and model review. Key properties: issue_type (structured: Clash, Coordination, Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change, Existing Condition, Client Feedback, Building Code, Other), trade (free text — NOT standardized), status, priority, rfi_header_id (links to RFIs). 17,999 design-typed issues across 327 companies. 4,967 linked to RFIs via rfi_header_id across 204 companies. Lifecycle: Identified → Under Review → Resolved → Closed.
Industry Standard: AIA E203; BIM Execution Plan coordination procedures; AGC BIMForum
Common Values: Clash, Coordination, Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change, Existing Condition, Client Feedback, Building Code, Other
Source Systems: Procore; BIM Track; BIM Collab; Navisworks; Solibri
Procore Coverage: High — coordination_issue table with structured issue_type, status, priority, and rfi_header_id linkage. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Compare coordination issue density (issues per $M) and resolution time by issue_type across project types
Notes: coordination_issue.trade is free text, NOT standardized ('Plumbing' vs '22 Plumbing'). MEP-heavy. Design-typed issues (Design Review, Constructability, Requirement Change) are a signal for design-related change impact (insight #31). Cross-domain: links to RFIs (Documents), change events (Financial Instruments).

Description: A regular team meeting where designers, trade contractors, and the GC review the federated model, resolve clashes, and make coordination decisions. Typically weekly during design and early construction. Decisions made in coordination meetings drive RFIs, change events, and revised drawings. Meeting records capture decisions and action items. The coordination meeting is the workflow — clash detection and coordination issues are the inputs/outputs.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (BIM coordination requirements); BIM Execution Plan meeting cadence; AGC coordination protocols
Common Values: Weekly coordination meeting, Discipline-specific coordination (MEP-only, structure-MEP), Prefab coordination session, Owner coordination review
Source Systems: Procore (meeting records); BIM Track; BIM Collab; Teams/Zoom recordings; meeting minutes
Procore Coverage: Medium — meetings tracked through Procore meeting tool (meeting, meeting_item tables). Coordination-specific meetings are a subset. Meeting items capture action items and decisions.
Benchmarking Use: Compare BIM coordination meeting frequency and action item closure rate across project types
Notes: Coordination meeting cadence and effectiveness directly predict field rework rates. Projects with weekly coordination have fewer field clashes than those with ad-hoc coordination. Cross-domain: meeting records (Documents), action items may become RFIs or coordination issues.

Category: --- REALITY CAPTURE: digital documentation of physical conditions ---

Description: 3D spatial data captured by terrestrial or mobile laser scanning — represents existing conditions or as-built geometry as millions of measured points. Used for: existing conditions documentation (renovation), as-built verification (comparing model to field), progress monitoring (scan-to-BIM), and quality control (dimensional tolerance checking). Scan-to-BIM workflows convert point clouds into model elements.
Industry Standard: ASTM E57 (3D Imaging Data Exchange); USIBD Level of Accuracy (LOA) Specification; AIA E203
Common Values: Terrestrial LiDAR scan, Mobile mapping scan, Handheld scan; Point density: 1mm–10mm typical; Registration accuracy: ±2mm
Source Systems: Leica (BLK360, RTC360); Faro (Focus); Trimble (X7, X12); Matterport; NavVis; processing software (Leica Cyclone, Faro Scene, CloudCompare)
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not store or process point cloud data. Scan data is managed in specialized software. Scan results may be referenced in observations or coordination issues.
Benchmarking Use: Compare laser scan adoption rates and scan-to-BIM conversion coverage across project types
Notes: Point clouds bridge the physical site to the digital model. As-built verification via scan-to-model comparison reveals construction quality and dimensional accuracy. Cross-domain: point clouds verify Building Elements, support as-built Documentation, and enable quality control.

Description: Spherical photographs taken at regular intervals throughout the project — creates an immersive, navigable visual record of site conditions over time. Captured on a regular schedule (daily, weekly, or at milestones) and organized by location and date. Enables remote site walks, progress tracking, and dispute documentation. The visual evidence layer for quality and safety records.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; company-specific documentation protocols
Common Values: Weekly capture, Milestone capture, Floor-by-floor documentation, Pre-cover documentation, Completion documentation
Source Systems: OpenSpace; Matterport; Holobuilder; Cupix; Procore (photo table); 360 cameras (Ricoh Theta, Insta360)
Procore Coverage: Medium — photo table captures photo records with location, date, trade, category. 360 photos stored as standard photos in Procore but without native 360 viewing or progress comparison features.
Benchmarking Use: Compare 360 photo documentation frequency and coverage (% of building captured) across project types
Notes: 360 photos are the fastest-growing reality capture method in construction — lower cost and faster than laser scanning. Linked to location (Locations domain) and time (Phases). Progress comparison (before/after) supports quality verification and dispute resolution.

Description: Aerial photography, videography, photogrammetry, and LiDAR data captured by unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) — provides site-level documentation, earthwork volume calculations, progress monitoring, and safety surveillance. Drone data includes orthomosaic maps (stitched overhead images), 3D terrain models, and volumetric calculations for cut/fill earthwork tracking.
Industry Standard: FAA Part 107 (commercial drone operations); company drone programs; ASTM standards for UAS in construction
Common Values: Orthomosaic (stitched aerial photo), 3D terrain model, Volumetric calculation (cut/fill), Progress photo/video, Thermal imaging, Safety surveillance
Source Systems: DJI drones; DroneDeploy; Propeller Aero; Skydio; Pix4D; Procore (photos uploaded from drone)
Procore Coverage: Low — drone photos uploaded to Procore as standard photos. No dedicated drone capture table or volumetric analysis. The analytical value (volumes, terrain models) lives in drone processing platforms.
Benchmarking Use: Compare drone capture frequency and earthwork volume tracking accuracy across project types
Notes: Drone captures are most valuable for site/earthwork projects (infrastructure, heavy civil) where volumetric tracking replaces manual survey. Cross-domain: earthwork volumes connect to Financial Instruments (pay-for-quantity) and Building Elements (Site & Earthwork).

Description: A standard photograph documenting site conditions, progress, quality issues, safety observations, or completed work — the most fundamental visual record in construction. Photos are attached to observations, punch items, inspections, daily logs, and RFIs as supporting evidence. Key properties: title, description, location, date, trade, category. Extremely high volume — thousands per project.
Industry Standard: Company documentation protocols; contract-required photography
Common Values: Progress photos (daily/weekly), Quality documentation, Safety documentation, Pre-cover photos, Completion photos, Issue documentation (attached to observations, punch items)
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; Fieldwire; BIM 360 Field; smartphone cameras; dedicated construction cameras (OxBlue, EarthCam)
Procore Coverage: High — photo table with structured metadata (title, description, location, date, trade, category). READY. Very high volume and adoption.
Benchmarking Use: Compare photo documentation rate (photos per day or per $M) across project types and companies
Notes: Photos are the visual evidence layer for nearly all UCDM record types. A single observation or punch item may have 5–10 attached photos. Photo volume and consistency indicate documentation discipline. Cross-domain: attached to Quality & Safety Records, Documents, Field Operations.

Category: --- VISUALIZATION & COMMUNICATION: model-based tools for planning and communication ---

Description: A 2D drawing view generated from or linked to a 3D BIM model — plans, sections, elevations, and details extracted from the model rather than drawn independently. Model-based drawings maintain a live link to the model — when the model changes, the drawing updates. This is the primary output mechanism for BIM during construction (contractors still work from 2D drawings, not 3D models). Drawing sheets in Procore track revisions to these outputs.
Industry Standard: NCS (National CAD Standard); AIA drawing numbering conventions; company sheet organization standards
Common Values: Plans, Sections, Elevations, Details, Schedules (door/window/finish), 3D views, Isometrics (MEP)
Source Systems: Revit (sheet views); AutoCAD; Bluebeam; Procore (drawing_revision table tracks issued sheets)
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision table tracks all revisions with discipline, number, revision number, and dates. Widely adopted. This is Procore's primary interface to the BIM model output.
Benchmarking Use: Compare drawing revision frequency and late-stage revision rate (post-IFC) across project types and disciplines
Notes: Model-based drawings are the bridge between 3D models and 2D construction documents. The drawing_revision table in Procore captures the output of BIM authoring — revision frequency by discipline reveals design stability. Cross-domain: drawings are referenced by RFIs, submittals, observations, and punch items.

Description: A living digital representation of the building that combines the as-built BIM model with real-time sensor data, maintenance records, and operational information — the post-construction evolution of BIM. Updated continuously during operations. Digital twins enable predictive maintenance, energy optimization, and space management. Currently an emerging technology with limited construction-phase application.
Industry Standard: ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Manufacturing); buildingSMART Digital Twin; ASHRAE BIM-to-FM guidelines
Common Values: Asset twin (equipment-level), Building twin (systems-level), Campus twin (portfolio-level); Static (as-built snapshot) vs. Dynamic (real-time sensor feeds)
Source Systems: Autodesk Tandem; Bentley iTwin; Microsoft Azure Digital Twins; Siemens; Willow; Invicara
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not support digital twins. The as-built model and COBie data from Procore's closeout process may feed INTO a digital twin platform, but the twin itself lives elsewhere.
Benchmarking Use: Compare digital twin adoption rates across building types and owner types
Notes: Digital twins are primarily an owner/facility management technology — construction's role is delivering the as-built model and data that feeds the twin. Cross-domain: digital twins consume data from Building Elements, Phases (commissioning), and Documents (O&M manuals). Adoption concentrated in healthcare, data centers, and large commercial.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs derived from Models & BIM domain entities ---

Description: Number of coordination issues per million dollars of project value — measures the volume of BIM-identified spatial and design conflicts relative to project size. Higher density may indicate more thorough coordination (good) or more design problems (bad) — context from resolution rate and issue_type distribution is needed for interpretation. Calculated: COUNT(coordination_issues) / (project total_value / 1,000,000). Segmented by project_type and issue_type.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; internal benchmarking
Common Values: Issues per $M; typical range varies widely by project type and coordination maturity
Source Systems: Procore (coordination_issue table)
Procore Coverage: High — coordination_issue table with structured issue_type, status, project linkage. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark coordination thoroughness and design quality across project types
Notes: Cross-domain: coordination issue density correlates with rework cost (Financial Instruments) and change event volume.

Description: Average days from coordination issue creation to resolution — measures how quickly spatial and design conflicts are resolved. Faster resolution = less downstream delay. Segmented by issue_type (Clash vs. Design Review vs. Constructability) and trade. Calculated: AVG(DATEDIFF(resolved_date, created_date)).
Industry Standard: No universal standard; internal benchmarking
Common Values: Days to resolution; typical range 3–30 days depending on complexity
Source Systems: Procore (coordination_issue table)
Procore Coverage: High — coordination_issue table has created_at and status timestamps. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark coordination workflow efficiency across project types and issue types
Notes: Long resolution times on Clash issues signal coordination bottlenecks. Design Review issues take longer by nature. Cross-domain: unresolved issues may escalate to RFIs (Documents) or change events (Financial Instruments).

Description: Percentage of identified clashes resolved before construction start — measures pre-construction coordination effectiveness. Higher rate = fewer field conflicts. Calculated: COUNT(coordination_issues WHERE status = 'Closed' AND issue_type = 'Clash' AND resolved_date < construction_start) / COUNT(coordination_issues WHERE issue_type = 'Clash'). Requires schedule phase data for cutoff.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; target >90% pre-construction resolution
Common Values: Percentage; industry best practice targets 90-95% pre-construction resolution
Source Systems: Procore (coordination_issue table, project schedule dates)
Procore Coverage: Medium — coordination_issue status and dates are available, but distinguishing pre-construction vs. during-construction resolution requires schedule phase integration not natively linked.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark pre-construction coordination quality and predict field rework risk
Notes: Unresolved pre-construction clashes predict field rework. Cross-domain: unresolved clashes → field conflicts → change events (Financial Instruments) → rework cost (insight #1).

Description: Number of drawing revisions issued per drawing set per month — measures design stability and churn. High revision frequency late in the project signals design instability and increases rework risk. Calculated: COUNT(drawing_revisions) / COUNT(DISTINCT drawing sets) / months. Segmented by discipline and project phase.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contract-defined revision cadence
Common Values: Revisions per drawing per month; typical range 0.5–3.0 depending on phase
Source Systems: Procore (drawing_revision table)
Procore Coverage: High — drawing_revision table tracks all revisions with discipline, number, and dates. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark design stability and identify high-churn disciplines across project types
Notes: Late-stage revisions (post-IFC) are particularly costly — they often drive RFIs and change events. Cross-domain: drawing revisions trigger RFIs (Documents) and change events (Financial Instruments).

Description: Number of construction photos documented per day or per million dollars — measures visual documentation discipline and thoroughness. Higher rates indicate more rigorous field documentation. Calculated: COUNT(photos) / construction_duration_days or COUNT(photos) / (total_value / 1,000,000). Segmented by project_type.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; company documentation policies
Common Values: Photos per day; typical range varies widely (10–100+ per day on active projects)
Source Systems: Procore (photo table)
Procore Coverage: High — photo table with structured metadata. Very high volume and adoption. READY.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark documentation discipline and identify under-documented projects
Notes: Photo documentation rate correlates with quality record completeness — projects with high photo rates tend to have better observation and punch item documentation. Cross-domain: photos attach to Quality & Safety Records and Documents.

Description: A composite measure of BIM coordination maturity based on: (1) whether coordination_issues exist, (2) issue volume relative to project size, (3) issue_type diversity (Clash + Design Review + Constructability), (4) resolution rate, (5) RFI linkage rate. Produces a score from 0-100 representing how thoroughly a project uses BIM coordination. Requires thresholds from benchmarking.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; internal maturity model
Common Values: Score 0-100; Low (0-30), Moderate (31-60), High (61-80), Advanced (81-100)
Source Systems: Procore (coordination_issue table, rfi linkage via rfi_header_id)
Procore Coverage: Medium — all component data is available in Procore, but the composite score requires derived calculation across multiple fields and benchmarking thresholds not natively computed.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark BIM coordination maturity across companies and project types
Notes: Composite score enables apples-to-apples comparison of BIM coordination usage. Companies with higher scores likely have lower rework rates. Cross-domain: aggregates data from coordination issues and RFIs to assess process maturity.
